title: 1. WELCOME
content:
WELCOME!# “Namaste.”
TLDR# We are high school seniors.
This book attempts to distill all the lessons we’ve learned in our years at high school. The material here embodies “what we wish someone had told us before we started high school.”
Why do we think you should read this? We think pointing out and solving some common pitfalls that high schoolers run into would prove useful in living the best high school life.
START# Here’s what we’re imagining: you’re sitting in a chair, maybe during the registry or lunch, and you’re thinking:
“Lol. Are they just doing it for the college app?”
No, we aren’t. There are a lot more “college efficient” activities than sitting down to write a book — tbh I think this is probably one of the most inefficient things you can do to get into a school.
Unless you’re truly exceptional, high school is a struggle: perhaps the classes could be hard, we don’t have friends/sleep, or we can’t seem to achieve our goals because we are addicted to scrolling/manga/YouTube. Or maybe it’s not a struggle yet. But trust us, problems are going to arise because you’re human. It’s like driving through SF’s 19th Avenue and trying not to have the car feel like a roller coaster (ok b4 they paved it.)
Because there’s a 99.99% chance that you could stumble in the many pitfalls — why would us seniors sit back when we can help our younger peers out?
So we are going to do what’s called a pro gamer move: reach out to our younger peers, point out pitfalls, and give our solutions/thoughts.
You see, we made mistakes, and one way to turn it into something positive is to tell you.
I, Justin, learned that when I could finally stomach my ego to ask my older peers for help, I could learn way more than I could do by myself. Not only that, I realized that many of them, especially those scary seniors, are actually really kind! Some guys, like Tyler, had the aura of a sigma. I swear, you can literally hear the sigma grindset theme when those guys are striding through crowds and halls! AND they really wanted to help out!
We can pick up where others left off! You can pick up the tools we’ve forged over the years, use them to soar even higher than us, and when it’s your turn to go, share your insights for the next generation!
This book is our attempt to distill all the lessons my peers and I have learned in our years at high school.
The material here embodies “what we wish someone had told us before we started high school.”
Our mission: make a lasting and positive difference for our younger peers.
Hey, we would clap our hands with delight if we learned that you have done something new and worthwhile from this book, and tried whatever “antics” we suggested.
Why do we think you should read this? We think pointing out and solving some common pitfalls that high schoolers run into would prove useful in living the best high school life.
Disclaimer# By the way, we aren’t here to tell you that you gotta claim the throne of “The AP King/Queen” by taking the most APs in US History. Or to “achieve” a sleep schedule of an alien — that is, a nonexistent one! We won’t sing hymns on how great you are. You already know you are. This book was designed and written to be as helpful as possible to you.
The book is not a collection of stories of past achievements. Instead, I have tried to organize and generalize the lessons of various experiences to produce a set of guidelines for our younger peers.
If you see anything profound here, assume it is from the experts/teachers we learned it from. If you see anything foolish, assume it is our error.
I know that not every book is perfect, and all of them succeed in some aspects and fail in others. So IF YOU HAVE A QUESTION, ASK!! There’s no way we can read your mind and slip answers on your bed. We don’t have the funds to slip your answers through your chimneys like Santa!
So what’s the best way to have the best high school experience?
We don’t know the golden formula.
But we do have some suggestions.
To that end, we offer this book.

title: 1.2 How to receive advice?
content:
How Do We Get New Maps?# TLDR# Maps, or paradigms, are powerful.
Good information shows us a more accurate map.
We can build better maps by having good connections with people and reading good books, among other ways.
Above all: use what we learn.
We shouldn’t hoard knowledge like we’re in a zombie apocalypse, because thoughts unrealized are wasted.
It’s incredibly difficult to understand something without actually using it, because life is often smarter than our ruminations.
Oh, we’re not ready? Do we need to know “just a little” more?
Nah.
We’re always going to feel unready.
No matter how much we know, improving always requires us to go out of our comfort zone, which is defined as the place where we aren’t comfortable.
We think this comfort zone idea is natural law, so we can’t cheat around it.
We know that effective change stems from changing our paradigm.
So how do we get new maps?
We can think of several ways:
1. Solo Grinding Our Way# Not recommended.
One way is to solo grind our way, but this is hard because we struggle to recognize our maps’ faults. Afterall, they look like reality!
I, Justin, don’t recommend this because I’ve tried to, and I could’ve learned so much more, way faster if I did options 2 and 3 instead.
What ends up happening is a lot of unproven theories and mediocre results.
It’s incredibly difficult to understand something without actually using it, because life is usually smarter than our ruminations.
But don’t get me wrong: spending time alone is great. But too much, and we start to delude ourselves.
Don’t be like Junior-year Justin. Instead use the wisdom from Thinking Fast and Slow – it is way easier for us to spot the faults of others. That’s where methods 2, 3, and 4 come in!
2. Making Great Connections# Recommended.
Making great connections is the first great alternative to adopt better paradigms.
Talk to goats → become a goat.
I think this is better than solo grinding because if we meet great people, then they will already make a great influence on us without us needing to conjecture any theories.
A great example — and a big regret of mine — was when my brother was effectively “handed down” the formula by his business professor for finding your way and dealing with distraction.
That was something I spent 3 years building on my own! Feels bad. (A modified version is in chapter 4.)
I felt jealous, yet happy there’s a better way to make better paradigms.
Yeah, people can really lift you up.
You are the sum of the people you meet, so if you walk with the wise, you become wise – that’s human psychology. I wish I could punch the spirit of human psychology, but life is life and I’m going to end up hurting myself if I try that again.
Hang out with the goats!
3. Reading Great Books# Recommended.
Reading great books is the second great alternative, for a very similar reason to making great connections.
We tend to become like the people we admire and hang around.
Books are one way people can “instill” themselves and their thoughts into us.
By reading, you are effectively spending time with the author by taking in their ideas.
Although books are wonderful — they changed my life, and I made a book recommendations list too — they don’t connect you as deeply as actually hanging out with people.
But books are still amazing because the authors have compressed their experience, insight, and suffering in a form you can absorb! Isn’t that sick?
4. Taking Action# Recommended.
Lastly, doing things and taking action.
Capitalize on the wisdom of life, because it is way smarter than whatever you’re ruminating about.
Don’t make decisions. As George Mack says, “remove the d-word from your dictionary”.
Make experiments.
Don’t feel finished when you think of something. Do something, however small it may be.
Pitfall: Eternal Student Syndrome# The first thing I, Justin, wished I knew was to not get trapped in a cycle of getting so much advice and not actually acting.
That itself is a downward spiral.
I guess another name for this trap is Eternal Student Syndrome.
This bears repeating:
Avoid the trap of being frozen by learning too much.
Isaac puts it this way:
Don’t let things rot in your mind.
Thoughts unrealized are thoughts wasted. (Ok please be safe though.)
You need to remember to keep doing 2, 3, and 4, with 1 only in moderation. It’s our choice to do so.
If you run into trouble setting up 2, 3, or 4, then you should read Chapter 2 or talk to trusted adults and friends.
Keep 1 around like a flame: make sure it stays alive, but don’t feed it so much that it burns you down.
Why Do We Fall Into This Trap?# What leads us to this trap?
How can we prevent it from “burning us down?”
One reason we might fall into this pitfall is because we never feel “ready,” or comfortable enough, to improve and actually start making changes.
But we realize that it’s very rare you’ll feel “ready” to use the advice we have here. Part of getting better is moving out of your comfort zone, which means getting out of the zone you’re comfortable in!
But what’s more dangerous about this trap?
It snowballs.
The more we freeze ourselves, the harder it is to feel ready, which makes it even more likely that we freeze ourselves deeper.
If we think we are not ready and end up not taking the shot, it’s going to affirm the identity that:
“You aren’t ready.”
Then you’ll look back and say:
“See? I wasn’t ready. Look at all the evidence!”
That is going to kill our motivation and end up making it even less likely to take shots.
And taking shots is one of the best ways to play high school.
Ok, What’s The Lesson?# No matter how much you know, actually making improvements means moving out of our comfort zones.
And moving out of our comfort zones means, by definition, being uncomfortable.
Look, it’s amazing you’re reading this and not doom-scrolling.
But don’t “doom-read,” or else you’re going to revert, then regret that you didn’t act.
If you feel unready most of the time, you aren’t alone.
Most people will instinctually hesitate and miss opportunities, even though those opportunities could pay off big time.
This permeated my high school years, and even those before us. (Even Shakespeare wrote a lot about this in Hamlet!)
It’s hard for our egos to admit, but if your map shows you that hoarding knowledge is better than using the knowledge to achieve results, you are headed for danger.
The Sports Example# Sports make this problem obvious.
If you hesitate too much, the consequence is obvious.
The opponent will move first, and very often, you’ll be at the mercy of your opponent.
Here’s a basketball example.
I only have a few moments to pull up a jumper before the opponent is able to block me.
If I hesitate:
“Oh, what if he’s going to block me?”
then the consequence is obvious:
I don’t get to shoot, and I will have to look for another opportunity.
In high school, the consequences of avoiding uncomfortable growth are not as obvious in the moment.
But they are very obvious when time goes on.
They are obvious to us seniors, so we want to tell you:
Don’t avoid the pains.
** set yourself up so that you won’t regret it later.**
You have wonderful potential, but only if you don’t shortcut or quick fixes.
The Right Map# We think this principle of being ok with moving out of our comfort zone is the right map. The more you obey it, the better your results will be.
Simple as that.
We can remember it like this:
“Growing involves moving out of your comfort zone.”
There’s a reason why some people improve faster than me.
One of the reasons is that they have accepted that being uncomfortable is part of improvement.
Paradigm Shift# So it’s not:
“Do I want to improve or not?”
Instead, it is:
“To be a little unready, or not to be?”
That is the question. (Thanks, Hamlet.)
The feeling of unreadiness is always going to be there, no matter how much you learn or prepare.
We can tell you everything we know, all our shortcomings, and all the lessons we’ve gotten from them.
But if you’re not even moving out of your chair, then what’s the point?
Too much research is just another form of procrastination, which leads, again, to unproven theories and mediocre results.
You won’t understand anything — including whatever words we tell you — unless you actually try it.
When you try something, you will obtain your own interpretation of whatever we say.
And that is 1000% easier to remember than passively reading. Again, from Thinking Fast and Slow, when we take action, we get experiences which have our emotions firing like crazy, which is one way we are hardwired to remember things!
This leads to…
Another Paradigm Shift# Knowledge isn’t power. The knowledge we implement is power.
We are here to tell you that there isn’t a yellow brick road leading you around.
We carve our own paths here.
To do that, use what we know, make experiments, and adjust bit by bit every day.
(More on that later in chapter 3.)
How to Use This Book# Use it.
Act on it.
Experiment with it.
We aren’t your English teachers testing you for how much you can remember.
FORWARD!

title: 2. Stuck overthinking? → Be proactive!
content:
2.0 Agency Problem? Be Proactive# TLDR# “My thoughts be bloody, or be nothing worth!”
— Hamlet
“We can always choose our response.”
— Victor Frankl
Being proactive means focusing on what we can control, accepting what we cannot, and choosing our actions based on our values instead of exploding like a diet-coke Mentos bottle rocket.
Life is too short for pointless fights.
Time spent kicking, yelling, and overthinking things outside our control is time not spent on things we can control.
A proactive person does not ask:
“Why didn’t life give me what I expected?”
They ask:
“It doesn’t matter what I expect of life. What does life expect of me?”
1. What Does It Mean to Be Proactive?# There are parts of life we cannot control.
We cannot change our spawn points like the Minecraft speedrunners. But…
we can control our response!
Being proactive means recognizing your agency in a situation and acting with intention.
It means making choices based on your values instead of reacting to every external impulse, insult, fear, or doubt. We are humans not diet-coke Mentos rockets that go crazy.
A reactive person says:
“This happened to me, so now I’m helpless AHHHH…”
A proactive person says:
“Ok this just happened. Why did this go wrong? What can I do next?”
You cannot always control how you feel, but you can always decide what to do next.
Either be the jailer of your own cell, or walk through your open prison door.
2. To Be Proactive Means to Focus on What You Can Control# It is easy to get stuck in thoughts like:
“What if I did this differently? If life was more fair?”
“BRO! When will everyone finally recognize that I’m the goat and start glazing me?”
Be warned! I tend to find that this tends to snowball on itself with its addictive fantasies! Ruminating on this leads to more fantasy, self-pity, or pointless fighting.
According to my experience, mulling over these thoughts is a waste of time.
A better practice is:
Worry about what you control.
Accept the rest.
Life can take opportunities from us.
Fate can close doors that might have made us better.
But what good is it to live inside imaginary alternate timelines or have conversations with people who don’t exist?
The past is a place to learn from, not a place to live in.
Give quality time for reflection, but not so much that it causes more problems.
3. When We Are Proactive, We Choose Our Responses# If someone says:
“I’m better than you at ___.”
We have a choice. (It helps to think of these dialogues like a turn-based game.)
Bogus response:
You can slug them. Why? They asked for it! RAHH!
Or you can carry that comment around all day:“AH someone hurt me! Waah (baby sounds)”
Or you can simply think:
“Okay.”
Then move on. We don’t need to fight unnecessary wars in our heads.
Yeah, a lot of energy is wasted because we take everything personally.
But if you fight tooth and nail over every little thing, you will be too tired for the things that actually matter.
4. Example: I Didn’t Get into Harvard# Say I worked extremely hard but did not get into Harvard.
I have a choice:
“This is so unfair. I should have gotten in. Everyone else is lucky. Everything I do is somehow wrong and everything they do is right.”
(Proceeds to cry.)
“This hurts, but life does not care what I expected. What does life expect of me now? I should learn from my mistakes, cheer on others around me, and keep moving forward.”
Doesn’t Option 2 sound way better? In fact, that sounds lwk chad!
We don’t pretend that disappointment doesn’t hurt.
We just don’t let it spill and make ourselves a mess. Just because we messed up a math problem doesn’t mean we need to tear it up, soak it in our tears, and eat it for dinner! We just storage it or recycle it.
5. Proactive Students Admit Their Ignorance and Ask Questions# This is especially powerful in AP classes, where many people suffer from Imposter Syndrome.
Let’s call that the “AP Imposter Syndrome.”
What does it sound like?
“That person looks like she knows everything.”
“Everyone will think I’m stupid if I ask.”
“I shouldn’t ask my question. It’s dumb.”
Don’t get me wrong. Yes, people in AP classes are smart.
But aren’t they taking the class because they don’t know everything?
Worrying about other people’s opinions is worrying about things we can’t control yet.
If we are proactive, we can choose to just ask our questions.
If you ask the question, here’s what happens:
The teacher teaches better.
Other students become less afraid to ask their questions too, which will benefit the whole class.
Know that regardless of how much value you get out of class, you are spending the same life force, your time and maybe money also, to attend class!
But if you ask questions, you might get 10x the value.
And those smart-looking classmates are probably not thinking about you, but shaking in their chairs from the same problem you’re facing. (More on this in the next chapter on fear, but you probably get the point.)
Isn’t that worth the shot?
6. Don’t Overthink It# Overthinking happens when the more you think, the harder the problem looks.
How do you know when you are overthinking?
A useful boundary I use is:
If I think something over more than three times before doing something, then I tell myself I’m probably overthinking.
Try this out!
At some point, doing the thing gives better information than thinking about it.
Sometimes, we can’t see the benefits until we get our head out of the loop.
7. Dealing with Black/White Thinking# Sometimes we get reactive because our emotions take over. This happens to me quite a lot!
It looks like this:
Before we do something we care about: “If this goes badly, everything is over.”
When getting insulted: “Everyone is out to get me. Don’t trust those bums.”
When messing up: “I SUCK! I’m the world’s greatest loser.”
When we succeed: “I’m the world’s greatest winner. The goat!” Then after celebrating: “That person did way better than me. I’m horrible!”
This makes us do worse actually, because we’re putting our attention on our negative thoughts and not what’s actually going on. We’re putting unnecessary stakes on ourselves, and it makes us our own nemesis when we get hurt.
We end up multiplying our own damage on ourselves, turning one point of damage into DOT damage! Isn’t it kinda weird to CHOOSE to hurt ourselves more?
And it’s hard to motivate ourselves when we are bogged down in these problems.
“Ok bro, how do we deal with this?”
We deal with this not by ignoring our emotions or suppressing them.
Instead, we should:
Recognize this type of thinking. Let it come like an old friend. Ask yourself “what is this emotion telling me?” Also, Journal it if helpful or Talk to trusted friends and adults. 8. Being Proactive Is Hard# Being proactive sounds simple, but it is easier said than done. What are some obstacles?
You are tired, like coming out of back-to-back tests. You do not know what the next step is because we’re in new territory. You have bottled-up negative feelings. (Important!) You distract yourself with devices instead of processing your emotions. (More in chapter 4!) So yeah, do not expect yourself to always enter main character mode through pure willpower. It takes quite a lot of energy/mana!
Here are our thoughts for each obstacle:
Push yourself and judge yourself fairly. Ask others for help. See Chapter 3 for more. Check out Section 7 above. Fix your environment. See Chapters 4 and 5. 9. Some Final Thoughts# Proactivity is not taking everything personally, not overthinking, and choosing our best actions.
Life will throw you garbage that’s outside of your expectations. When you are there, ask yourself:
“What does life expect of me?”

title: 2.1 Afraid? --> Shift POV and just do something!
content:
Afraid? Shift POV and Just Do Something!# TLDR# The problem: we hesitate when faced with the opportunity to grow.
This fear hurts us because we may end up missing the opportunity, along with the many benefits it could’ve brought us.
So how should we deal with this?
One way is to look at it from a new perspective. Shift pov!
Here’s a suggestion for a new map:
What’s true is that you’re excited. Know that our bodies can’t tell the difference for some biological reason. Nobody cares as much as you think. It’s natural to be scared of new things. We can’t shut down this caveman brain’s fight-or-flight reaction, but we can still choose to move forward! Know that our bodies lie to us sometimes. We don’t need to be the jailer of your own cell.
You do not need to feel confident before acting.
Action builds confidence, so if you’re asking “Oh, what if I fail?” We’ll just say
Nah stopping yapping. Do it and let’s talk after.
I like this quote a lot:
“Are you in earnest? seize this very minute—
What you can do, or dream you can, begin it,
Boldness has genius, power, and magic in it!
Only engage, and then the mind grows heated—
Begin it, and the work will be completed!”
— Goethe
Good guideline: if you’ve thought three times before doing something, but “I’m afraid” is the main reason you’re not doing it, that means you should probably just do it.
START# Problem: we hesitate when faced with the opportunity to grow.
This fear hurts us because we end up missing the moment and the many benefits it could’ve brought us.
So how should we deal with this?
A solution: shift your view.
Use a new map:
What’s true is that you’re excited. Your body can’t tell the difference for some reason. Nobody cares as much as you think. It’s natural to be scared of new things. Know that our bodies lie to us sometimes. We don’t need to be the jailer of your own cell.
In other words, what you see or think isn’t always reality, so don’t trap yourself in your own ruminations.
Speaking will come once you get started – it’s an upward spiral!
Fear Is Like a Cold Toilet Seat# I wish I knew to treat fear like sitting on a toilet seat.
The toilet seat is usually very cold and, honestly, a bit uncomfortable for the first five or so seconds before it starts to heat up and feel fine.
But there’s no way I can do something important — in this case, using the bathroom — unless I sit through the few seconds of a cold seat.
Sometimes, you just have to accept that you will almost always feel a bit of fear. But we also need to accept that we still have the choice to move forward.
Our natural instinct is to make it complicated and end up not doing anything.
But what is a good definition of complicated?
Well, I think something is complicated if I needed to think it over three times.
If, in all my 3 attempts, “I’m afraid” is the main reason I’m not doing something, that means I should just do it, because it means I’m facing the toilet scenario from above.
Of course, use your common sense when doing this.
Don’t be reckless, and stay safe.
Please don’t misinterpret this and think:
“Oh, if I try to cheat, there might be magic!”
Erm, I don’t think so.
Some Reasons Why We Fear# We’re afraid of being “different.” We think we don’t know enough, it’s too hard, or we need more “practice.” We think we might “lose” something. Let’s deal with these one at a time.
1. We’re Afraid of Being “Different”# Most people are not watching you that closely.
They are mostly busy with their own lives and problems and won’t really remember that you even attempted something.
So even if you fail, they won’t really care.
Remember, we make it as hard as we imagine it to be! And because our society is made of humans, they are probably all thinking the same thing. That could be reassuring.
2. We Think We Don’t Know Enough# Action helps you gather more information more efficiently than overthinking.
Besides, a completed action beats a perfect action that never happens.
Also, just getting your ideas out can actually help you think better.
For some odd psychological reason, if we just take initiative, then confidence will follow.
I personally barred myself from pursuing opportunities by fooling myself with:
“I’m practicing dude! Just need a little bit more time…”
But by the time I’m 100% ready, usually the opportunity has already passed.
And what hurts more is that I tell myself I’ll get it next time, calm myself down with “all is well,” then proceed to miss another opportunity.
Don’t do that.
3. We Think We Might “Lose” Something# Sometimes, we are afraid because our ego will tank if we fail/succeed.
Or we might lose our aura, or our status as being “The Smart.”
But in reality, our opinions may be wrong and our expectations actually don’t matter most of the time.
PS: The only way you can lose is by not doing something.
When I went to my school’s math club, I was really scared to ask for help.
I was so self-conscious about how others would treat me differently, and that I won’t be the “smart-glasses-hoodie dude in the corner” as I appeared to be.
And to this day, I am still quite scared to ask for help, even as the math president!
But sometimes, fear will always stare you down.
We just have to stare back at it, know that it’s always going to be there, and just start moving.
In my math class, I always get super nervous when I admit my absurd levels of ignorance.
But hey — I’m still alive, and I’ve learned a lot more than if I had not taken any shots.
Our “loss” is just a stubborn illusion.
Growing Means Moving Out of Your Comfort Zone# Recall from Chapter 1.2: seizing the opportunities we are given will always make us uncomfortable.
Growing means moving a little bit out of our comfort zones.
Remember, even if you fail, they won’t care.
And, which is even cooler, they’ll celebrate with you when you succeed!
PS: > Most people don’t care about what you think or do.
So if you fail, it doesn’t matter because they don’t care.
Why don’t they care?
They have enough problems of their own.
If you do succeed, then people actually celebrate with you.
Kinda crazy, no?
Silence Does Not Make You Cool# Sometimes, we think we look cool if we are sitting there thinking and doing cool gestures. Besides, everyone is doing it too.
But looking back, it’s silly.
Lots of us have been doing this for a little more than three years, and we’ve gained close to nothing from being silent.
When I didn’t ask for help during class, there were many times where I ended up having to grind my head off to understand a concept that I could’ve gotten within ten seconds of asking my teacher or classmates for help.
This is also a good study tip: (more in chapter 5)
Allow the teacher to teach you by asking questions.
Remember that no one cares what you are doing, even if you ask a “dumb” question.
And by the way, when you step up, others will follow, and it becomes easier for you to keep going.
As Goethe said, boldness has magic in it.
Some Potential Obstacles# Unfortunately, we suffer the “easier said than done” curse, like in the previous chapters. Elisa points out some potential obstacles:
!) classrooms are not always oriented to encourage conversation.
We fear rejection from trying to talk to someone we find interesting. When things don’t turn out the way you wanted, stay proactive.
And get support also! Good friends and mentors make it easier to implement the ideas we’ve discussed.
Dealing with the AP Class Scenario, AKA Imposter Syndrome# Almost everywhere, but especially in AP classes, we think our peers are so smart.
But in reality, they are taking the class because they are not so smart.
Often, people only seem smart to others because they don’t do enough for you to see their mistakes.
Practice:
Just ask a question or answer once. Take small steps if necessary.
Besides, if you’re thinking that so-and-so is going to think you’re awkward, the reality is:
Nobody cares!
We just have to accept that fear always takes a seat on your shoulder to whisper random fantasies in your mind.
We must also accept that it is up to us to move forward and act.
Notice# We place our fear in the right places.
There’s nothing to fear except for being unkind, dishonest, etc.
We fear not being the best person we could be.
But sometimes, we could be proactive in the wrong place.
So the next chapter answers:
“How do you know that?”
Because then, if we know we are doing something wrong, we can be proactive and change ourselves.

title: 4.2 Building and Breaking Habits
section: 4. Procrastinating? Put the first things first.
content:
4.2 Building and Breaking Habits# (Note)
This section is based on what I learned from Atomic Habits.
I see this section as a generalized version of Chapter 4.1. I wrote about dealing with scrolling first because I thought it would be more useful.
Anyways…
TLDR# The repetition of behavior, not the sincerity of belief, is what shapes character. You become the kind of person who does the things you repeatedly do.
Habits have a loop:
Cue Craving Response or routine Reward If you want to make a habit, make it:
Obvious Attractive Easy Satisfying If you want to break a habit, make it:
Invisible Unattractive Difficult Unsatisfying You can cut the loop anywhere, and then you cannot execute the habit as easily.
Bad habits repeat themselves not because you do not want to change, but because you have the wrong system for change.
You do not rise to the level of your goals, but fall to the level of your systems.
Small changes often appear to make no difference until you cross a critical threshold.
The most powerful outcomes of any compounding process are delayed. You need to be patient.
Context Matters More Than We Think# We do not like to think this, but a lot of our habits depend on our context. Environment is op!
If all our friends are chilling, we will almost always feel at least some urge to go chill too.
If we are in a library where everybody is locked in, like during AP test season, we will almost always feel the urge to lock in too.
What matters is that you stick with something.
Make sure you actually get reps in before you start optimizing.
The Atomic Habits Rules# The habit loop is:
Cue → Craving → Response/Routine → Reward
To make a habit, make it:
Obvious Attractive Easy Satisfying To break a habit, make it:
Invisible Unattractive Difficult Unsatisfying Example: Dealing With Justin’s YouTube Bingeing# First, let’s assess why I had a habit of bingeing YouTube.
What was actually going on?
As we know from above, every habit is formed by its loop:
Cue Craving Response/Routine Reward Let’s examine each one.
Cue# Watching YouTube was very obvious to me because I was constantly using the internet, and struggling with difficult studies is uncomfortable. My struggle became a cue to hop on Youtube!
Craving# Since I was uncomfortable, I wanted to get rid of that discomfort.
So I would look around for quick relief – hey Youtube is just a few buttons away! I want to watch videos so I can escape.
Response# I’m uncomfortable + YouTube is nearby, so I did:
Command + T → y → YouTube autofills → Enter
By that point, my fingers could do the full motion in a snap.
Reward# Watching YouTube was satisfying.
It was satisfying to see a new cat video, a street chef cooking, pro gamers smoking their competitors, or new speedrun tech. After each video, an even cooler video popped up, and that was another cue to watch another.
Why This Became a Habit# This became a habit because it was:
obvious Attractive Easy Satisfying a. It was super obvious, because I already watched YouTube a lot, so every time I opened a browser to study, I knew there was a very high chance I would end up on videos.
b. It was attractive, especially when I was grappling with hard concepts from class. (It was also incredibly easy to lose sight of my goals once I started watching those engaging videos. And I also genuinely like watching professional athletes and mathletes do their craft.)
c. It was absolutely easy; literally four buttons away.
d. But the weird part is that it was not actually that satisfying. It was just a little more satisfying than studying, which was enough to get me started.
Then I would feel horrible afterward, and that created a downward spiral:
Discomfort → Video → Regret → More discomfort → Another video
Yeah, this did not go well for me.
So How Did I Break This?# If we want to break a habit, we make it:
Invisible Unattractive Difficult Unsatisfying I attacked all four stages of the loop. My solution is not perfect, but it still helps me.
Hopefully, it can help you too.
Make It Invisible# To make YouTube invisible, I minimized the chances that I would be alone with my device.
When I made time for solitude, I asked my mom or friends to hide my device until I got my work done.
Make It Unattractive# I had to convince myself that watching YouTube was bad for me, even though it feels great in the short term.
I told myself that bingeing videos made my problems worse, and I would not want to be remembered for bingeing videos. (See the parts “Objectives” and “Problems” form the previous section.)
Make It Difficult# This part was hard.
I installed extensions that blocked recommendations.
I then turned off my watch history so I would not get jumped by a horde of wonderful videos screaming for my attention whenever I opened YouTube.
Make It Unsatisfying# To make YouTube unsatisfying, I maximized the chances that I could get caught.
For me, that meant going to a professional environment like SF State’s library or a teacher’s office.
If I got caught by a teacher, friend, or a smart-looking student, I would feel at least a little queasy, because they’ll shoot a “are you serious?” glance!
That tanked my ego, so the thought of watching YouTube did not feel as great anymore.
The Biggest Lesson# This might be a bit of a tangent, but the biggest thing I learned from dealing with my bad YouTube habit was this:
I should not have done it all by myself.
If I had asked others how they dealt with this, and if I had consulted books earlier, I probably would have found this solution, or an even better one, in half the time.
Remember, you are not alone. Leverage the people around you.
Even if they are not geniuses, they can grow with you. And that makes way more efficient and fun.
EXTRA: Atomic Habits Practices# If you would like some nice practices from Atomic Habits, here you go.
I highly recommend the book, by the way.
The 1st Law: Make It Obvious# 1.1 Fill Out the Habits Scorecard# Write down your current habits to become aware of them.
1.2 Use Implementation Intentions# Use this format:
“I will [BEHAVIOR] at [TIME] in [LOCATION].”
1.3 Use Habit Stacking# Use this format:
“After [CURRENT HABIT], I will [NEW HABIT].”
1.4 Design Your Environment# Make the cues of good habits obvious and visible.
Inversion of the 1st Law: Make It Invisible# 1.5 Reduce Exposure# Remove the cues of your bad habits from your environment.
The 2nd Law: Make It Attractive# 2.1 Use Temptation Bundling# Pair an action you want to do with an action you need to do.
2.2 Join a Culture Where Your Desired Behavior Is Normal# Join a group where the behavior you want is already the normal behavior.
2.3 Create a Motivation Ritual# Do something you enjoy immediately before a difficult habit.
Inversion of the 2nd Law: Make It Unattractive# 2.4 Reframe Your Mindset# Highlight the benefits of avoiding your bad habits.
Ask yourself what kind of person you want to become.
One possible exercise:
Imagine your funeral. What do you want people to remember you for?
The 3rd Law: Make It Easy# 3.1 Reduce Friction# Decrease the number of steps between you and your good habits.
3.2 Prime the Environment# Prepare your environment to make future actions easier.
3.3 Master the Decisive Moment# Optimize the small choices that deliver outsized impact.
3.4 Use the Two-Minute Rule# Downscale your habits until they can be done in two minutes or less.
3.5 Automate Your Habits# Invest in technology and one-time purchases that lock in future behavior.
Inversion of the 3rd Law: Make It Difficult# 3.6 Increase Friction# Increase the number of steps between you and your bad habits.
3.7 Use a Commitment Device# Restrict your future choices to the ones that benefit you.
The 4th Law: Make It Satisfying# 4.1 Use Reinforcement# Give yourself an immediate reward when you complete your habit.
4.2 Make “Doing Nothing” Enjoyable# When avoiding a bad habit, design a way to see the benefits.
4.3 Use a Habit Tracker# Keep track of your habit streak.
Do not break the chain.
4.4 Never Miss Twice# When you forget to do a habit, make sure you get back on track immediately.
Inversion of the 4th Law: Make It Unsatisfying# 4.5 Get an Accountability Partner# Ask someone to watch your behavior.
4.6 Create a Habit Contract# Make the costs of your bad habits public and painful.
Onwards!

title: 4. Procrastinating? Put the first things first.
content:
4.0 Procrastinating? Put First Things First# TLDR# Being able to put first things first is needed if you want to get good work done.
We can make it easier by setting ourselves up to avoid our internal battles.
Some good suggestions:
clarify the task and why you care about it choose a good environment that’ll help not hinder focus on one thing at a time (try not to multitask) write out your excuses take small and consistent steps (These ideas are inspired by 7 Habits, Deep Work, and Digital Minimalism.)
The Problem# When we have important tasks, we might proceed like this:
“I’m going to get all my homework done!” 2)But then the distractions come in, like that new YouTube video, or our friends sit next to us and have fun, or we start multitasking with something that popped up.
And when our delegated word expires, we stare at the ceiling in disappointment (“bro! I didn’t get anything done!”
This happened to us a lot, so what do we think? How can we
not procrastinate avoid distractions and focus on the important things and not something else? We are not advocating for an anti-fun policy or declaring that we must become a hermit if we want to get our stuff done. Who likes that?
But when we need to get something done, we should avoid distracting ourselves as much as possible until we finish. Oh what about “just muster up that discipline!”? – isn’t consistent enough to be reliable. More on that later.
So What Are Some Good Methods?# Focus on prioritizing tasks throughout the day instead of relying only on rigid time schedules. When we prioritize something, we do it as if our life depended on it (so that means we should ONLY do that, right?) We find it easier to plan by setting priorities each day/week, but we also have to admit that we cannot have everything at the same time. (Like why would you stuff your backpack full of books when we realistically have time for 1? I killed my back for the presence of more books, and I didn’t get any smarter lol.)
Multiple times per day, you may need to weigh the benefits of focused work against the other stuff. Actually writing it down helps, because it’s easier to spot bogus reasons on paper than in thoughts. A Simple Example# “Write college applications at the library after school.”
Then focus on one thing at a time.
No multitasking, because it’s a horrible way to work.
Or…
Locking in for homework during class time, then talking with friends afterward, is better than half-doing homework while yapping with friends.
To be honest, half-doing homework while talking with friends looks really nice.
But I know my grade probably will not.
Willpower Alone Is Not Enough# Relying on willpower alone does not work consistently.
If you tell yourself:
“Oh, I just need a little bit more discipline, then I will get it done.”
Then be prepared to face the mirror at night and realize the whole day passed without anything getting done.
Yeah, I feel you. That has happened an embarrassingly high number of times to me.
“Well, why doesn’t it work?”
Our daily willpower varies, and it is limited: some days we feel like going full sicko mode.
On the other hand, when we are tired because we’ve used a lot of attention throughout the day, that’s called ego depletion. Our willpower goes poof!
So is there a better way for using our willpower?
Use willpower for things that actually matter. Block off unnecessary things so we don’t have to burn it fighting out distractions.
(It’s like saving your heavy mana-cost spells/ults by using auto attacks on those weaker enemies!)
Avoid the Battle Before It Starts# If we are constantly battling to focus, our easier habits will eventually win over the harder but more beneficial habits; that’s known as the law of least effort (Deep Work.) That’s the unfortunate reality.
It is not worth using a bunch of willpower to fight off distractions that could have been avoided beforehand.
So how can we avoid unnecessary distractions before we get tempted? How can we AVOID, not fight it? (Or, in gaming terms, how can we use our auto attacks?)
1. Make Your Priorities Clear# Make it clear what your priorities are. Then clarify why something is important to you, because a big YES to what is important helps you say no to things that aren’t.
So ask yourself:
“What actually matters right now?”
A good reason you can use is:
“In my funeral, do I want myself to be remembered for this?”
Let’s go deeper:
Start with “Why am I doing this?”# When I’m tempted to procrastinate, I like to ask myself:
“Why would I choose to put my most important task at the mercy of something less important?”
And also:
“If this really was important to me, why would I choose to hurt myself? By not doing it?”
That question hurts a little, but it works. It reminds me that procrastination is not just “being lazy.”
Sometimes it is choosing something less important over something I actually care about. And like the Muller-Lyer Illusion, it still feels right to get distracted, but hey we know the truth!
Well, what else can we do to help ourselves?
2. Go to a Better Environment# Now that you know that what you’re doing actually matters (or not), automate your focus by going to a good environment.
Let the environment deal with the distractions, not your tired, worried, afterschool self! (From Models of the Mind, this method of letting smth external deal with the trouble is called externalization.)
For example:
Lock in during class or work in a library.
When your environment supports the thing you want to do, you do not need to fight as hard.
A good environment makes the right action easier; a bad environment makes the wrong action easier. Again from above, choose an environment that’ll deal with the distractions so that our weak selves don’t need to do it.
3. Focus on One Thing at a Time# Do not try to do homework, text people, watch videos, and philosophize about the meaning of life at the same time.
That is usually fake productivity, and it never worked for me.
If you need to focus, remove the distractions before you start.Throw your phone out the window before you get to work. Ok, maybe do not actually throw it out the window. But you get the point.
Do that thing ONLY. Talk later after you’re done. If you have thoughts that come up either ignore it or write it down quickly and get back; don’t ride on the internal roller coaster of thoughts when you haven’t finished what you need to do.
A Useful Anti-Procrastination Exercise# This exercise has helped me a lot.
Ask yourself:
What have I been procrastinating on?
Why?
What is stopping me now or later?
What is something I can do right now?
Final Reminder# You do not beat procrastination by only yelling at yourself to have more discipline.
You beat it by making the important thing clear, choosing a better environment, and focusing on one thing at a time.

title: 5.0 Struggling or Getting Stuck?
content:
5.0 Struggling or Getting Stuck?# Study Tips: Cover your bases by not procrastinating and be extra proactive in class.# TLDR# Cover your bases. Do what the teacher tells you to do before you try to do your own stuff. Don’t procrastinate on this!
This is gonna sound so cliche, but that’s because it’s SO true: Do your homework, sleep well, and be extra proactive in class (that means ask and answer questions, really try to do what the teacher says and beyond)
Out of class, focus is good because it helps you get quality practice, but do not try to solo everything. Heed Newton’s advice:
“If I have seen further, it is by standing on the shoulders of giants.”
Pay attention to your environment; find out what actions it rewards you for (that is, the baseline to succeed in the class), then either adapt or find a new environment. For example, ask yourself if the class rewards you for deeply understanding the formulas or just memorizing them for application.
Don’t get disappointed! Problem solving requires patience, perseverance, and accepting that being stuck is part of the process.
The Problem# Classes are sometimes tedious and other are straight-up mental acrobatics.
And sometimes, we run into the classic “I’m so cooked” scenario:
We have an essay due tomorrow, and we have not started.
Oh, we also have a math test tomorrow.
And this physics equation looks like some gobbledegook of Greek letters.
Oh, and there is also a horde of finals right before AP exams.
How do we deal with this?
Well first, we must understand the problem…
Why This Happens# Let’s take a step back and look at the causes of this distressingly common scenario.
Some common causes are:
Wrong view of creativity Fear of struggle Procrastination (quick note: I’m going to say “read chapter 4.0”, because I’ve literally made a dedicated chapter for that!) Here are some solutions:
Put in consistent effort and communicate with others Struggle just enough, and learn to move on Bro, just read chapter 4 on procrastination 1. Wrong view of creativity – Instead of Trying to Be Creative, Put In Consistent Effort and communicate with others# You will get inspired and have those creative “aha!” moments if you are patient and put in consistent effort. Most of us aren’t brilliant enough to consistently rely on those bright flashes of inspiration to survive in hard classes.
Our school environment has wonderful resources, like teachers, friends, and even that random dude in the corner. Use them. If they are mean to you doesn’t mean you should shy away from them: either learn to not take it personally or go find someone else.
Trying to solo everything drains so much energy, so use other people’s ideas and feedback to learn in addition to your focused practice.
Author Tiago Forte says this well:
“The myth of a writer sitting down before a blank page, or the artist on a completely blank canvas, is just that—a myth. Professional creatives constantly draw on outside sources of inspiration: their own experiences and observations, lessons gleaned from successes and failures alike, and the ideas of others. If there is a secret to creativity, it is that it emerges from everyday efforts to gather and organize our thoughts and ideas.”
Newton is known for his genius works, and yet even Mr Newton also admits that he did not do it alone, but through the efforts of others:
“If I have seen further, it is by standing on the shoulders of giants.”
This does not mean you should neglect your solo time. Experiment with yourself to find how much time you like alone/working with other minds (like referencing books, people, etc…) Just know the value of being consistent and
2. Fear of struggle – Struggle Enough, Then Learn to Move On# For most of us, it is common to despair when we struggle with hard problems.
Fear not.
Struggling is not failing, but is actually a crucial part of learning! (This is especially true for hard physics classes, like physics C [the best class tbh, I actually needed to get physical with those equations.])
Andrew Wiles, winner of a Fields Medal (Nobel Prize for math), says that struggle can feel like failure, but it is not. He points out that a good mathematician (or problem solver in this case) has “patience, perseverance, and acceptance.”
Problem solving = patience, perseverance, and accepting that being stuck is part of the process.
We should challenge ourselves, but we should not overwhelm ourselves by taking gigantic steps. Don’t be like Justin who spent weeks on a single problem just to find out that the solution was just pure greek (it was a horribly inefficient way to improve my skills, though my ego would still try to gaslight me.) Struggling is part of learning, and so is learning to move on from problems that are too hard.
When we spend too long on a problem to the point that we fall behind, it can be smart to cut our losses and move on. After covering our bases, we can return to it and ask about that tough question.
Sometimes, it is okay to give up, learn the solution, and then try again later after you have caught up with class.
It is not worth rabbit-holing on a single problem in exchange for your grades.
Prelearning# Prelearning is a great tactic, especially in harder classes. It goes like this:
Before your class goes over something, go read up on it. Warning! Make sure to not rabbit-hole too hard if you do not understand something. Instead, write down what you do not understand and ask the teacher when the moment comes.
make a general map of the new stuff. Things like important definitions and concepts.
Then in class, we focus on filling the gaps and asking questions. If the teacher offers challenging problems, we can take advantage of this because we aren’t stuck with the basic concepts.
Problem-Solving Tip: Start Simple Before Going Complex# Start with the simple before the complex.
Isn’t it way more efficient to get the kill with a one-damage punch rather than using your Final Ultimate: Collapsing Star Roaring Cannon Mega Ultra… (plus ten more intimidating adjectives?)
We have limited time! Only do the crazy manipulation when the simple stuff don’t work! Doing the complex before the simple is called overthinking.
Solving a problem is like checking what tools you actually have before sprinting down Complexity Road toward Genius Gymnasium on Delusion Drive.
Cramming? Prioritize the Big Rocks First# (Should read chapter 4 for this, below is just extra stuff. Still helpful hopefully!)
Cramming happens when we force effort into a short amount of time, even though that effort should have been spread out over a longer period.
To deal with this, consider this question:
Have you ever tried fitting big rocks and small rocks into a container?
If you put the small rocks in first and then the big rocks, the small rocks use up a lot of space. But if you put the big rocks in first and then the small rocks, the small rocks fit nicely between the big rocks. This uses the space much better.
Fitting rocks is similar to managing our daily tasks.
A lot of the time, we end up cramming our big rocks at the end because the small rocks have eaten up the day.
We should treat our important tasks like big rocks and less important tasks like small rocks.
Big rocks before small rocks.
That way, we do not need to cram as much.
Also: Cramming and pulling all-nighters do not work well long-term. Just don’t all-nighter, even if you think you’re the goat.
Procrastination and Distraction? Prioritize Environment Over Willpower# You might say:
“But I can’t help but have my small rocks eat up my time! I don’t have that discipline!”
That is why we should prioritize having a great environment over relying on constant self-discipline.
A good environment favors the things you want to do. If you want to party, go somewhere like a beach. If you want to focus, go to a library. (Though studying at a beach does not sound too bad…)
A well-chosen environment can be so strong it can even make boring things seem fun.
On the other hand, a bad environment quietly destroys your effort, even if 10 seconds before you were lock and loaded to get stuff done. It wastes your time and energy because we could easily take away these troubles BEFORE they come up!
So please, do not lie to yourself when you pretend you are focused in a horrible environment.
A great application of this is when you consider heading home to work.
Experiment with this:
Do not go straight home if home kills your focus.
It is common for us to see the bed or computer and have our motivation crash out.
Stay realistic. Work where you work well.
It also doesn’t hurt to experiment with different locations!
Digital Environments Matter Too# For digital environments, it helps to avoid temptation rather than confront it.
Remember from chapter 4.1?
“tbh, those engineers are just too smart at hooking you into your feed. Don’t even bother fighting it!”
Not now, maybe fight it later if you’d like. But please don’t let it hinder your dreams!
As for practices, I block recommendation feeds and move to a quiet classroom to get my work done.
Let Teachers Help You# Lastly, choose to let teachers help you. I REALLY wish I used this more often. **There’s a reason we have teachers, people who have devoted their life to make your life easier (or harder?)
A good teacher saves you time.
They can offer valuable feedback and point you in the right direction so you do not wander around in confusion while trying to understand the basic material. They are amazing!
It is hard to grind alone and decode material by yourself.
It is supposed to be like that.
And that is why we have teachers!
Final Summary# School gets overwhelming when we procrastinate, isolate ourselves, fear struggle, and mismanage priorities.
But with a good environment, consistent effort, smart prioritization, and the right attitude toward being stuck, learning becomes much more manageable, and even a bit addictive just because it gets so FUN.

title: 6.0 Tired? Rest Properly
content:
6.0 Tired? Rest Properly# TLDR# Sleep is not optional. Sleep is goated.
If you are resting, that does not mean bingeing on your device. Again, it’s just not worth fighting those engineers who devoted their lives to hook you!
Real breaks should help us recover, not trap us.
Some good suggestions: pre-plan your breaks and environment before you work, making the breaks social is good also.
Sleep Either Helps You or Breaks You# Why We Sleep is a 368-page book with one very clear message:
Sleep or suffer. Seriously!
Dr. Walker, the author, has literally spent his life to tell us to sleep! The book uses evidence from all sorts of science to show how powerful sleep is.
Yeah, I think we should sleep well.
Good sleep helps memory, learning, mood, focus, and health. Bad sleep hurts all of those.
You will not do well in school if you do not sleep. Unless you are one of those truly gifted people, then be my guest.
It is usually not worth pulling an all-nighter just to study more or finish more homework. Ok, I actually don’t have much experience with pulling all-nighters to finish homework, but it’s just… you get the point, hopefully.
Be honest with yourself: If your cramming system leaves you tired, stressed, inconsistent, and unable to sustain your work, then it probably is not the way to move forward.
It is recommended to sleep at least eight hours, though you can adjust based on yourself.
Some people may say “oh, my body will adjust.” That might feel true, but getting used to bad sleep does not mean bad sleep is okay. For more reasons why we should sleep, you can also read Walker’s book Why We Sleep. Or you can just heed this advice and just sleep well!
Extra (you can skip this if you want!): Experiment With Waking Up Early# Experiment with whether waking up early fits you well.
Taking a Block 0 may or may not help you. If you do well waking up early, it could be useful.
But it could also cut into your natural sleep, especially because adolescents’ natural sleep schedules tend to shift later.
If it does not hurt your sleep, waking up earlier can help you focus more easily, because the morning is very quiet. Which makes it much easier to focus with fewer distractions.
Still:
Do not destroy your sleep.
Okay, you probably get the point.
We should sleep properly.
But what about resting during the day?
Using Entertainment? Do Not Binge Your Device# DO NOT BINGE YouTube, TikTok, or other platforms to rest/take a break. I can’t believe how many times I told myself “oh I’m just going to watch one video”, and then I end up falling prey to the beautifully addictive algorithms of Youtube! (More in the next section.)
What’s recommended? Go hang out with friends. Boredom can also be a nice way to rest if you’re alone.
Remember from chapter 1.2: A fun and positive crowd can make life much better.
For example, choir can be a really fun crowd. I used to mistake that kind of group as lazy, but looking back, it was actually goated. I really wish I had stayed instead of bingeing YouTube videos alone in the room.
The YouTube Break Trap# When I used YouTube to take breaks, I was basically walking into a trap.
Here is what usually happened to me:
“I’ll watch one video.”
Ten minutes pass.
“Okay, break ended—oh, that video looks really good!”
Then 20 minutes pass. 40. I’m tired now.
Worse, I feel guilty because I just messed up my focus AND my time.
Ok if you really want to use entertainment, make sure it brings you closer to people you care about, like playing games only with your siblings.
The best thing we can do when taking breaks is to not get trapped when you use it.
I kinda like this–When planning how to take a break, I ask myself:
“Will this help me come back stronger, or make it harder to return?”
Again:
Breaks should not become indulgence.
Uh Oh! What If I Already Doomscrolled?# We lose control and scroll. Then we suddenly realize we should have been grinding.
It is like the moment your mom opens the door and you get jumpscared harder than when playing Five Nights at Freddy’s. We don’t need to go berserk mode, because that gets tiring in the long term.
I like to gently re-enter motion by starting with simple things:
For the next few minutes, usually around five minutes, I do something easy. I get up and organize my things.
This helps me restart without needing a huge amount of willpower/rage.
Experiment: Try Out Boredom# Sometimes, getting some time to be bored helps us recover.
Experiment with yourself.
But make sure boredom does not turn into brain rotting.
Be honest about whether your “rest” is actually restoring you or just numbing you.
Try out different suggestions and notice what actually helps.
Make Breaks Social When You Can# I went through a phase where I tried to make even my breaks “efficient.”
I practiced pen spinning, juggling, mental math, and working out during breaks because I wanted to be the type of person who could do hard math problems and cool tricks because who knows why.
The issue was that I was alone for too long.
Now this might not generalize for all of us, but studying alone in a secluded place, then taking a break alone to practice some skill, was TIRING!
By the time I had to return to work, I often felt even more frustrated. For example, I was upset that I kept failing that really cool pen spin 20 times, then when the timer rang I didn’t want to get back to work.
Earlier, I said we can experiment with being bored.
The reason I add “make sure you do not end up brain rotting” is because **when we are tired after a focused session, it is hard to resist temptation. **(Remember 4.1? Ego depletion!)
This is especially true when we are alone.
So I suggest:
Make breaks social when possible. Go laugh with friends who were also locked in.
Other people (and things too) can steer our behavior for better or for worse. That’s a reason why we must…
Prepare Your Environment Before You Work# Set up your future self for success. He/she will be tired.
And remember when you are tired, you are easier to distract (due to ego depletion.)
That means you are also more likely to binge.
This is where a pre-mortem (post-mortem, but beforehand) helps. Ask yourself:
“What might go wrong?”
Before committing to a focused session, I ask myself:
“What in my environment will help me or distract me?”
Then I remove the possible problems before they become obstacles.
Examples:
Put my phone away. Give my phone to my friends. Clean unnecessary things off my desk. Move to the best spot I can find. Prepare my materials. Refill my lead so that I don’t have to get up and end up joining the TikTok scrolling army. There is a quote that sums this up:
“Victorious warriors win first, then go fight the battle.”
Our battle of focus is easier when our environment helps us.
Some More Break Suggestions# Try these when you need a real break:
Walk around. Talk or laugh with friends. Journal. Clean your space. Run errands. Sit quietly for a few minutes. Good Rules for Rest, Summary# Sleep well.
Make breaks social when possible.
Avoid breaks that turn into addiction.
Prepare your environment before working.
If you mess up and doomscroll, rebuild momentum with something easy.
